translation: If you work, you will receive a prize.
options: open conditional
unfulfilled conditional (present)
unfulfilled conditional (past)
temporal clause
explanation: sī + present indicative (labōrās) + future indicative (accipies) is an open conditional — it presents a real, possible condition with no implication it is unfulfilled. The indicative in both clauses signals openness: the working may or may not happen, but the condition is treated as genuinely possible.

translation: If my friend were coming (but he isn't), I would be very happy.
options: unfulfilled conditional (present)
open conditional
unfulfilled conditional (past)
temporal clause
explanation: sī + imperfect subjunctive (veniret) + imperfect subjunctive (essem) is an unfulfilled conditional in present time — sometimes called 'contrary to fact (present)'. The imperfect subjunctive signals that the condition is not currently being met: the friend is NOT coming, therefore the happiness is not happening.

translation: If the soldiers had not fled, we would have won.
options: unfulfilled conditional (past)
open conditional
unfulfilled conditional (present)
temporal clause
explanation: nisi (if not/unless) + pluperfect subjunctive (fugissent) + pluperfect subjunctive (vicissēmus) is an unfulfilled conditional in past time. The pluperfect subjunctive signals both clauses refer to a past situation that did not happen: the soldiers DID flee, so we did NOT win. nisi = sī nōn in most cases.

translation: If you drink water, you will be healthy.
options: open conditional
unfulfilled conditional (present)
temporal clause
unfulfilled conditional (past)
explanation: sī + present indicative (bibis) + future indicative (eris) is a simple open conditional — a genuine possibility. The drinking of water and resulting health are treated as real possibilities, not counter-factual. Indicative in both clauses = open, real condition.

sentence: sī Rōmae nātus essem, Latīnē melius loquerer.
display: sī Rōmae nātus essem, Latīnē melius loquerer.
translation: If I had been born in Rome, I would speak Latin better.
options: unfulfilled conditional (present)
open conditional
unfulfilled conditional (past)
causal clause
explanation: sī + pluperfect subjunctive (nātus essem — the birth is in the past) + imperfect subjunctive (loquerer — the speaking is present). This is a 'mixed' unfulfilled conditional: past condition (was not born in Rome) with present consequence (would speak better now). The imperfect subjunctive in the main clause confirms the present unfulfilled result.

sentence: sī pecūniam habēbis, librum emere poteris.
display: sī pecūniam habēbis, librum emere poteris.
options: If you have money, you will be able to buy the book.
If you had money, you would be able to buy the book.
If you had had money, you would have been able to buy the book.
Since you had money, you were able to buy the book.
correct: If you have money, you will be able to buy the book.
explanation: sī + future indicative (habēbis) + future indicative (poteris) is an open conditional. Latin uses the future in the sī clause (while English uses the present: 'if you have'). Both verbs in the indicative confirm this is a real, open condition — it may well happen.

options: If you had said this, you would have been wrong.
If you were saying this, you would be wrong.
If you say this, you will be wrong.
Since you said this, you were wrong.
correct: If you had said this, you would have been wrong.
explanation: sī + pluperfect subjunctive (dīxissēs) + pluperfect subjunctive (errāvissēs) is an unfulfilled conditional in past time. Both pluperfect subjunctives confirm this is about a past situation that did not occur: you did NOT say this, so you were NOT wrong. Completely counter-factual.

sentence: nisi Cicerō adfuisset, rēs pūblica periisset.
display: nisi Cicerō adfuisset, rēs pūblica periisset.
options: If Cicero had not been present, the republic would have perished.
If Cicero were not present, the republic would perish.
Since Cicero was not present, the republic perished.
If Cicero is not present, the republic will perish.
correct: If Cicero had not been present, the republic would have perished.
explanation: nisi (if not) + pluperfect subjunctive (adfuisset) + pluperfect subjunctive (periisset) is an unfulfilled past conditional. The implication: Cicero WAS present, so the republic did NOT perish. Both pluperfect subjunctives point to a past counter-factual scenario.

sentence: sī omnēs cīvēs cōnsentirent, pāx fierī posset.
display: sī omnēs cīvēs cōnsentirent, pāx fierī posset.
options: If all the citizens were to agree, peace could be made.
If all the citizens had agreed, peace could have been made.
If all the citizens agree, peace will be able to be made.
Since all the citizens agreed, peace was made.
correct: If all the citizens were to agree, peace could be made.
explanation: sī + imperfect subjunctive (cōnsentirent) + imperfect subjunctive (posset) is an unfulfilled conditional in present time — they are NOT currently agreeing, so peace cannot currently be made. fieri is the present passive infinitive of faciō (to be made); posset is imperfect subjunctive of possum.

options: If we had not hurried, we would have missed the ship.
If we were not hurrying, we would miss the ship.
If we do not hurry, we will miss the ship.
Because we had not hurried, we missed the ship.
correct: If we had not hurried, we would have missed the ship.
explanation: sī nōn (if not, alternative to nisi) + pluperfect subjunctive (festīnāvissēmus) + pluperfect subjunctive (āmīsīssēmus) is an unfulfilled past conditional. The implication: we DID hurry, so we did NOT miss the ship. āmittere nāvem = 'to miss/lose the ship'. Both pluperfect subjunctives signal the past counter-factual.
